report_name: TrapDoor Supply Chain Attack Steals Developer Credentials Across Multiple Open-Source Registries

attack_patterns.name: Compromise Software Dependencies and Development Tools
attack_patterns.description: The core of the TrapDoor attack involves publishing over 34 malicious packages to public repositories like npm, PyPI, and Crates.io, which execute malicious code upon installation or build.

attack_patterns.name: JavaScript
attack_patterns.description: The `trap-core.js` payload, a key component of the TrapDoor malware, is executed by the npm `post-install` hook, leveraging the JavaScript interpreter to run on the victim's system.

attack_patterns.name: Credentials from Web Browsers
attack_patterns.description: The TrapDoor malware actively searches for and steals browser data, including cookies, history, and stored passwords.

attack_patterns.name: Credentials In Files
attack_patterns.description: The TrapDoor malware scans the host system for credentials stored in local application and system configuration files, such as AWS credentials, GitHub tokens, and private SSH keys.

attack_patterns.name: SSH
attack_patterns.description: The TrapDoor malware uses stolen private SSH keys to attempt lateral movement to other systems accessible from the compromised developer machine.

attack_patterns.name: Cron
attack_patterns.description: The TrapDoor malware creates cron jobs as one of several methods to establish persistence on the host system.

attack_patterns.name: Systemd Service
attack_patterns.description: The TrapDoor malware creates systemd services to establish persistence on compromised host systems.

attack_patterns.name: Web Protocols
attack_patterns.description: Used for C2 communication and data exfiltration, with reports indicating the use of GitHub Gists as a covert channel.

attack_patterns.name: LLM Prompt Injection
attack_patterns.description: The TrapDoor malware injects hidden Unicode characters into configuration files (`.cursorrules`, `CLAUDE.md`) to trick AI coding assistants like Claude and Cursor into executing malicious scripts for data discovery and exfiltration.

malware.name: TrapDoor
malware.description: A sophisticated, multi-repository supply chain attack malware that targets developers to steal credentials, cryptocurrency wallets, and other sensitive secrets. Its core payload, `trap-core.js`, is executed via package manager hooks and performs credential discovery, lateral movement, persistence, and data exfiltration.

tools.name: Claude
tools.description: AI coding assistant abused by the TrapDoor malware. The malware injects hidden Unicode characters into configuration files to trick Claude into executing a malicious script for data discovery and exfiltration.

tools.name: Cursor
tools.description: AI coding assistant abused by the TrapDoor malware. The malware modifies its configuration files (.cursorrules) with hidden instructions to execute malicious scripts for data discovery and exfiltration.

campaigns.name: TrapDoor Campaign
campaigns.description: A sophisticated, multi-repository supply chain attack campaign targeting developers across npm, PyPI, and Crates.io to steal credentials and cryptocurrency wallets, starting around May 22, 2026.
campaigns.objective: Steal developer credentials, cryptocurrency wallets, and other sensitive secrets from the cryptocurrency, DeFi, and AI sectors.

identities.name: npm
identities.description: One of the targeted open-source package registries where over 34 malicious packages were published as part of the TrapDoor campaign.

identities.name: PyPI
identities.description: One of the targeted open-source package registries where malicious packages were published as part of the TrapDoor campaign.

identities.name: Crates.io
identities.description: The Rust package registry targeted by the TrapDoor campaign, with malicious packages using `build.rs` scripts for execution.

identities.name: Coinbase
identities.description: Cryptocurrency exchange whose users' wallets were targeted for theft by the TrapDoor malware.

identities.name: Binance
identities.description: Cryptocurrency exchange whose users' wallets were targeted for theft by the TrapDoor malware.

identities.name: MetaMask
identities.description: Cryptocurrency wallet targeted for theft by the TrapDoor malware.

identities.name: Brave
identities.description: Web browser with an integrated cryptocurrency wallet that was targeted for theft by the TrapDoor malware.

identities.name: AWS
identities.description: Cloud provider whose access keys and tokens were targeted for theft by the TrapDoor malware.

identities.name: GitHub
identities.description: Version control platform whose personal access tokens were targeted for theft. Its Gists feature was also abused as a C2 and data staging channel.

infrastructure.name: GitHub Gist C2 Channel
infrastructure.description: The TrapDoor malware was reported to use GitHub Gists as a covert channel for command and control communications and for staging exfiltrated data.

indicators.name: TrapDoor Malicious Package Names
indicators.x_netsecio_sigma_rule:
title: TrapDoor Malicious Package File Creation
id: 2b1a9c8d-f3e4-4b5a-8c7d-6e5f4a3b2c1d
status: experimental
description: Detects the creation of files associated with the TrapDoor supply chain attack, which uses malicious packages on npm, PyPI, and Crates.io.
references:
  - https://www.rescanatech.com/blog/trapdoor-supply-chain-attack
author: cyber.netsecops.io
date: 2026-05-26
tags:
  - attack.t1195.001
level: high
logsource:
  category: file_event
  product: linux
detection:
  selection:
    TargetFilename|endswith:
      - '/eth-security-auditor'
      - '/dev-env-bootstrapper'
      - '/trap-core.js'
  condition: selection
indicators.description: Detects file names associated with malicious packages used in the TrapDoor supply chain attack.

indicators.name: TrapDoor AI Assistant Config Modification
indicators.pattern:
title: TrapDoor AI Assistant Configuration File Modification
id: 9e8d7c6b-5a4b-4c3d-2e1f-0a9b8c7d6e5f
status: experimental
description: Detects modification of AI assistant configuration files (.cursorrules, CLAUDE.md), a technique used by the TrapDoor malware to inject malicious instructions.
references:
  - https://www.rescanatech.com/blog/trapdoor-supply-chain-attack
author: cyber.netsecops.io
date: 2026-05-26
tags:
  - attack.aml.t0051
level: medium
logsource:
  category: file_event
  product: linux
detection:
  selection:
    TargetFilename|contains:
      - '/.cursorrules'
      - '/CLAUDE.md'
  condition: selection
indicators.description: Detects the modification of AI assistant configuration files, a novel technique used by TrapDoor to execute malicious scripts.

indicators.name: TrapDoor C2 and Exfiltration via GitHub Gists
indicators.pattern:
title: TrapDoor C2 and Exfiltration via GitHub Gists
id: 8d7c6b5a-4b3c-4d2e-1f0a-9b8c7d6e5f4a
status: experimental
description: Detects POST requests to GitHub Gists, which may indicate data exfiltration or C2 communication used by the TrapDoor malware. This could be noisy in developer environments.
references:
  - https://www.rescanatech.com/blog/trapdoor-supply-chain-attack
author: cyber.netsecops.io
date: 2026-05-26
tags:
  - attack.t1071.001
  - attack.t1041
level: medium
logsource:
  category: proxy
detection:
  selection:
    cs-host|endswith: 'gist.github.com'
    cs-method: 'POST'
  condition: selection
indicators.description: Detects network traffic patterns indicative of TrapDoor using GitHub Gists for command and control or data exfiltration.

relationships.target_name: Compromise Software Dependencies and Development Tools

relationships.source_name: TrapDoor C2 and Exfiltration via GitHub Gists
TrapDoor C2 and Exfiltration via GitHub Gists
